branch: Passenger Protect Program and Targeting Operations
description:
PACT aims to identify and apply risk mitigation measures to inbound high-risk
air shipments that could contain concealed improvised explosive devices or
other threat items prior to loading and departure to Canada. This is achieved
through the risk assessment of advanced cargo information from air carriers
with the assistance of emerging technologies. After exhausting manual
methods for data analysis, the PACT pilot was selected by the Deputy
Minister’s Committee on Policy Innovation in 2018 as a test case to determine
if the use of advanced analytics powered by artificial intelligence (AI) could
help manage the workload. During the 2018-2019 fiscal year, a proof of
concept was carried out which demonstrated that these emerging
technologies could indeed be applied to the categorization and risk
assessment of advanced cargo information (i.e. shipper and consignee name
and address, cargo weight, description and piece count, and air waybill
number). The PACT system accepts industry standard messaging in the form
of digital air waybills which contain all eight required data elements. With
respect to interpreting free text fields in airwaybills – a data field that is rife
with typos and industry jargon – the ability of the technology to categorize the
results effectively and efficiently within a risk assessment framework made
the expansion of PACT possible.
The PACT system will sort and categorize the data and apply a risk algorithm
to flag potentially high-risk shipments to the PACT analyst. The shipments
whose characteristics fall within a pre-defined low risk scenario are auto-
assessed and are not presented to a PACT analyst for review. Conversely, the
shipments that fall outside these parameters are presented to PACT analysts,
who then make a decision to apply a risk mitigation measure to an air cargo
shipment prior to it being loaded on an aircraft destined for Canada. These
take the forms of Requests for Information (RFI), Requests for Screening
(RFS) or Do Not Loa
qa_section_text:
Section 3.1: Impact Questions and Answers
Reasons for Automation
1. What is motivating your team to introduce automation into this decision-making process?
(Check all that apply)
Improve overall quality of decisions
Lower transaction costs of an existing program
The system is performing tasks that humans could not accomplish in a
reasonable period of time
Use innovative approaches
2. What client needs will the system address and how will this system meet them? If possible,
describe how client needs have been identified.
Overall, industry is looking for a seamless and digital means of sending
advanced cargo information and receiving status updates and referrals in a
timely manner. In addition to providing a secure means of shipping, air
carriers are committed to the provision of an expedient service to their clients
(including individuals and businesses seeking to ship cargo to Canada).
Air carriers and data aggregators have been directly involved in the system
development to establish a data format and means of transmission that are
aligned with industry standards. Extensive industry consultation has been
undertaken during the PACT pilots since 2012 and the views that were
recorded have long informed the program design (including the system
parameters). Feedback has stressed the importance of expediency in risk
assessments to minimize operational impacts on shippers and other supply
chain partners.
Primary users of the system (Transport Canada employees) have been
involved in its design and testing to ensure it is user-friendly and meets the
business need: a system that can receive, sort, risk and display results from a
large volume of data from various sources through a web API.
3. Please describe any public benefits the system is expected to have.
By enabling the implementation of the PACT program, the system will benefit
the travelling public and air industry workers by adding a layer of security to
the air cargo supply chain, which includes both passenger and all-cargo
flights.
4. How effective will the system likely be in meeting client needs?
Very effective [ Points: +0 ]
5. Please describe any improvements, benefits, or advantages you expect from using an
automated system. This could include relevant program indicators and performance targets.
The benefit of the automated system is the expediting of a risk assessment
process that adds a layer of security to all inbound air cargo shipments.
Regular shippers of legitimate cargo should not be adversely affected by the
implementation of the PACT Program as air carriers will receive system-
generated status updates on their shipment data.
The system is expected to improve the "messages targeted per hour" rate by
approximately ten times. It is also expected to capture 100% of the air carriers
that fly to Canada and assess 100% of the data. Currently, only 6% is risk
assessed using manual methods and based on a limited number of
volunteers.
6. Please describe how you will ensure that the system is confined to addressing the client
needs identified above.
Transport Canada employs a robust Project Management Framework which
ensures business leads articulate the project scope across various project
artefacts, which are referred to over the course of the project and altered (as
necessary) through change requests as part of the project’s governance. The
scope includes a specific dataset that follows a pre-defined industry standard.
This is verified as part of an onboarding process to ensure only relevant data
is transmitted. The system is restricted to specific users on the Transport
Canada intranet and each user’s role is reflected in their permissions. A
training program tailors the material to the trainee’s role in the system.
7. Please describe any trade-offs between client interests and program objectives that you have
considered during the design of the project.
The system was initially configured to realize efficiencies that could not be
realized under a manually operated regime. The deployment of a
technological solution was the only means to provide a meaningful layer of
security for inbound air cargo. Any Pre-Loading Advanced Cargo Information
(PLACI) program without such an advanced technological solution would
create gross inefficiencies along the supply chain. As such, no trade-offs were
considered.
8. Have alternative non-automated processes been considered?
Yes [ Points: +0 ]
9. If non-automated processes were considered, why was automation identified as the preferred
option?
The PACT pilot began using Microsoft Outlook and Excel, and with a limited
number of volunteers, the maximum capacity of the software was reached. In
addition, when we extrapolated on the capacity of our existing staff to
determine what we'd need to target all inbound shipments, it was not feasible.
Automation is not preferred; it's necessary.
10. What would be the consequence of not deploying the system?
Service cannot be delivered at all [ Points: +3 ]
Risk Profile
11. Is t

Algorithmic Impact Assessment Results
Version: 0.10.0
Project Details
1. Name of Respondent
Carrothers, Brook
2. Job Title
Manager
3. Department
Transport (Department of)
4. Branch
Passenger Protect Program and Targeting Operations
5. Project Title
Pre-load Air Cargo Targeting (PACT) Program
6. Project ID from IT Plan
N/A
7. Departmental Program (from Department Results Framework)
Pre-load Air Cargo Targeting (PACT) Program
8. Project Phase
Implementation [ Points: 0 ]
9. Please provide a project description:
PACT aims to identify and apply risk mitigation measures to inbound high-risk
air shipments that could contain concealed improvised explosive devices or
other threat items prior to loading and departure to Canada. This is achieved
through the risk assessment of advanced cargo information from air carriers
with the assistance of emerging technologies. After exhausting manual
methods for data analysis, the PACT pilot was selected by the Deputy
Minister’s Committee on Policy Innovation in 2018 as a test case to determine
if the use of advanced analytics powered by artificial intelligence (AI) could
help manage the workload. During the 2018-2019 fiscal year, a proof of
concept was carried out which demonstrated that these emerging
technologies could indeed be applied to the categorization and risk
assessment of advanced cargo information (i.e. shipper and consignee name
and address, cargo weight, description and piece count, and air waybill
number). The PACT system accepts industry standard messaging in the form
of digital air waybills which contain all eight required data elements. With
respect to interpreting free text fields in airwaybills – a data field that is rife
with typos and industry jargon – the ability of the technology to categorize the
results effectively and efficiently within a risk assessment framework made
the expansion of PACT possible.
The PACT system will sort and categorize the data and apply a risk algorithm
to flag potentially high-risk shipments to the PACT analyst. The shipments
whose characteristics fall within a pre-defined low risk scenario are auto-
assessed and are not presented to a PACT analyst for review. Conversely, the
shipments that fall outside these parameters are presented to PACT analysts,
who then make a decision to apply a risk mitigation measure to an air cargo
shipment prior to it being loaded on an aircraft destined for Canada. These
take the forms of Requests for Information (RFI), Requests for Screening
(RFS) or Do Not Load (DNL) notices.
About The System
10. Please check which of the following capabilities apply to your system.
Text and speech analysis: Analyzing large data sets to recognize, process, and
tag text, speech, voice, and make recommendations based on the tagging
Risk assessment: Analyzing very large data sets to identify patterns and
recommend courses of action and in some cases trigger specific actions
Content generation: Analyzing large data sets to categorize, process, triage,
personalize, and serve specific content for specific contexts
Process optimization and workflow automation: Analyzing large data sets to
identify and anomalies, cluster patterns, predict outcomes or ways to
optimize; and automate specific workflows
Section 1: Impact Level : 2
Current Score: 49
Raw Impact Score: 49
Mitigation Score: 36
Section 2: Requirements Specific to Impact Level 2
Peer review
Consult at least one of the following experts and publish the complete review or a plain language
summary of the findings on a Government of Canada website:
qualified expert from a federal, provincial, territorial or municipal government institution
qualified members of faculty of a post-secondary institution
qualified researchers from a relevant non-governmental organization
contracted third-party vendor with a relevant specialization
a data and automation advisory board specified by Treasury Board of Canada Secretariat.
OR
Publish specifications of the automated decision system in a peer-reviewed journal. Where
access to the published review is restricted, ensure that a plain language summary of the
findings is openly available.
Gender-based Analysis Plus
Ensure that the Gender-based Analysis Plus addresses the following issues:
impacts of the automation project (including the system, data and decision) on gender and/or
other identity factors;
planned or existing measures to address risks identified through the Gender-based Analysis
Plus.
Notice
Plain language notice posted through all service delivery channels in use (Internet, in person,
mail or telephone).
Human-in-the-loop for decisions
Decisions may be rendered without direct human involvement.
Explanation
In addition to any applicable legal requirement, ensure that a meaningful explanation is provided
to the client with any decision that results in the denial of a benefit or service, or involves a
regulatory action. The explanation must inform the client in plain language of:
the role of the system in the decision-making process;
the training and client data, their source, and method of collection, as applicable;
the criteria used to evaluate client data and the operations applied to process it;
the output produced by the system and any relevant information needed to interpret it in the
context of the administrative decision; and
a justification of the administrative decision, including the principal factors that led to it.
Explanations must also inform clients of relevant recourse options, where appropriate.
A general description of these elements must also be made available through the Algorithmic
Impact Assessment and discoverable via a departmental website.
Training
Documentation on the design and functionality of the system.
IT and business continuity management
None
Approval for the system to operate
None
Other requirements
The Directive on Automated Decision-Making also includes other requirements that must be met
for all impact levels.
Link to the Directive on Automated Decision-Making
Contact your institution's ATIP office to discuss the requirement for a Privacy Impact
Assessment as per the Directive on Privacy Impact Assessment.
Section 3: Questions and Answers
Section 3.1: Impact Questions and Answers
Reasons for Automation
1. What is motivating your team to introduce automation into this decision-making process?
(Check all that apply)
Improve overall quality of decisions
Lower transaction costs of an existing program
The system is performing tasks that humans could not accomplish in a
reasonable period of time
Use innovative approaches
2. What client needs will the system address and how will this system meet them? If possible,
describe how client needs have been identified.
Overall, industry is looking for a seamless and digital means of sending
advanced cargo information and receiving status updates and referrals in a
timely manner. In addition to providing a secure means of shipping, air
carriers are committed to the provision of an expedient service to their clients
(including individuals and businesses seeking to ship cargo to Canada).
Air carriers and data aggregators have been directly involved in the system
development to establish a data format and means of transmission that are
aligned with industry standards. Extensive industry consultation has been
undertaken during the PACT pilots since 2012 and the views that were
recorded have long informed the program design (including the system
parameters). Feedback has stressed the importance of expediency in risk
assessments to minimize operational impacts on shippers and other supply
chain partners.
Primary users of the system (Transport Canada employees) have been
involved in its design and testing to ensure it is user-friendly and meets the
business need: a system that can receive, sort, risk and display results from a
large volume of data from various sources through a web API.
3. Please describe any public benefits the system is expected to have.
By enabling the implementation of the PACT program, the system will benefit
the travelling public and air industry workers by adding a layer of security to
the air cargo supply chain, which includes both passenger and all-cargo
flights.
4. How effective will the system likely be in meeting client needs?
Very effective [ Points: +0 ]
5. Please describe any improvements, benefits, or advantages you expect from using an
automated system. This could include relevant program indicators and performance targets.
The benefit of the automated system is the expediting of a risk assessment
process that adds a layer of security to all inbound air cargo shipments.
Regular shippers of legitimate cargo should not be adversely affected by the
implementation of the PACT Program as air carriers will receive system-
generated status updates on their shipment data.
The system is expected to improve the "messages targeted per hour" rate by
approximately ten times. It is also expected to capture 100% of the air carriers
that fly to Canada and assess 100% of the data. Currently, only 6% is risk
assessed using manual methods and based on a limited number of
volunteers.
6. Please describe how you will ensure that the system is confined to addressing the client
needs identified above.
Transport Canada employs a robust Project Management Framework which
ensures business leads articulate the project scope across various project
artefacts, which are referred to over the course of the project and altered (as
necessary) through change requests as part of the project’s governance. The
scope includes a specific dataset that follows a pre-defined industry standard.
This is verified as part of an onboarding process to ensure only relevant data
is transmitted. The system is restricted to specific users on the Transport
Canada intranet and each user’s role is reflected in their permissions. A
training program tailors the material to the trainee’s role in the system.
7. Please describe any trade-offs between client interests and program objectives that you have
considered during the design of the project.
The system was initially configured to realize efficiencies that could not be
realized under a manually operated regime. The deployment of a
technological solution was the only means to provide a meaningful layer of
security for inbound air cargo. Any Pre-Loading Advanced Cargo Information
(PLACI) program without such an advanced technological solution would
create gross inefficiencies along the supply chain. As such, no trade-offs were
considered.
8. Have alternative non-automated processes been considered?
Yes [ Points: +0 ]
9. If non-automated processes were considered, why was automation identified as the preferred
option?
The PACT pilot began using Microsoft Outlook and Excel, and with a limited
number of volunteers, the maximum capacity of the software was reached. In
addition, when we extrapolated on the capacity of our existing staff to
determine what we'd need to target all inbound shipments, it was not feasible.
Automation is not preferred; it's necessary.
10. What would be the consequence of not deploying the system?
Service cannot be delivered at all [ Points: +3 ]
Risk Profile
11. Is the project within an area of intense public scrutiny (e.g. because of privacy concerns)
and/or frequent litigation?
No [ Points: +0 ]
12. Are clients in this line of business particularly vulnerable?
No [ Points: +0 ]
13. Are stakes of the decisions very high?
Yes [ Points: +4 ]
14. Will this project have major impacts on staff, either in terms of their numbers or their roles?
No [ Points: +0 ]
15. Will the use of the system create or exacerbate barriers for persons with disabilities?
No [ Points: +0 ]
Project Authority
16. Will you require new policy authority for this project?
No [ Points: +0 ]
About the Algorithm
17. The algorithm used will be a (trade) secret
No [ Points: +0 ]
18. The algorithmic process will be difficult to interpret or to explain
No [ Points: +0 ]
About the Decision
19. Please describe the decision(s) that will be automated.
The decision that is automated is whether to allow shipments to proceed
without interference based on a combination of risk indicators. The PACT
advanced algorithm will generate an inbound cargo risk score for each
shipment based on specific parameters. Based on this score shipments that
have pre-defined low-risk characteristics will be automatically assessed;
whereas those that fall outside those parameters will be reviewed by a human
case-by-case.
20. Does the decision pertain to any of the categories below (check all that apply):
Other (please specify) [ Points: +1 ]
21. Please describe
Aviation security - air cargo security
Impact Assessment
22. Which of the following best describes the type of automation you are planning?
Partial automation (the system will contribute to administrative decision-
making by supporting an officer through assessments, recommendations,
intermediate decisions, or other outputs) [ Points: +2 ]
23. Please describe the role of the system in the decision-making process.
The system will assist PACT analysts in risk assessing shipments by
displaying relative risk ratings with relevant information such as whether the
shipment follows a known trade pattern. Shipments that have pre-defined low-
risk characteristics will be automatically assessed; whereas those that fall
outside those parameters will be reviewed by a human case-by-case.
24. Will the system be making decisions or assessments that require judgement or discretion?
Yes [ Points: +4 ]
25. Please describe the criteria used to evaluate client data and the operations applied to
process it.
Air carriers submit a mandatory dataset to Transport Canada (shipper and
consignee name and address, cargo weight, description and piece count, and
air waybill number) and each field is checked for data accuracy (e.g. expected
character length, character type, etc.). Specifically, natural language
processing and fuzzy matching are used to interpret free text fields in the air
waybill to clarify and group the commodity descriptions into meaningful
categories (e.g. jeans/denim/pants = clothes). The system then applies an
automated risk algorithm that calculates a score. Shipments that fall above a
risk threshold are presented for the user for further risk analysis. Targeters
review the results and use subject matter expertise to clear the shipment or
apply a risk mitigation.
26. Please describe the output produced by the system and any relevant information needed to
interpret it in the context of the administrative decision.
Outputs of the system are: an inbound cargo risk score displayed to PACT
analysts for each shipment, and electronic messages sent to the air carrier.
The inbound cargo risk score delineates those shipments that require further
review from those that do not.
The electronic messages let the air carrier know if the data is complete,
assessed, or flagged for an RFI, RFS, or a DNL notice. There are two possible
automated messages sent by the system when shipment data is received:
error codes are sent automatically when incomplete data is received by the
system, and assessment complete messages are sent automatically for low-
risk shipments once they have been processed by the advanced algorithm.
Status messages are sent once a human targeter has made a determination
on shipments that fall above the low-risk threshold. These status messages
can confirm that the assessment is complete or indicate the need for the air
carrier to implement an identified mitigation action.
27. Will the system perform an assessment or other operation that would not otherwise be
completed by a human?
Yes [ Points: +2 ]
28. If yes, please describe the relevant function(s) of the system.
The system will receive, sort and apply an algorithm to a volume of data that
the team was not able to process using available tools such as Microsoft
Excel.
29. Is the system used by a different part of the organization than the ones who developed it?
No [ Points: +0 ]
30. Are the impacts resulting from the decision reversible?
Likely reversible [ Points: +2 ]
31. How long will impacts from the decision last?
Impacts are most likely to be brief [ Points: +1 ]
32. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
When a request for screening is made, the hope is that the screening reveals
there is no concealed improvised explosive device or other threat item, and
the shipment can proceed. In the worst-case scenario (where the shipment is
prevented from being loaded because of an unresolved flag), the impact is
isolated to the singular shipment (e.g., to one box or one pallet) and would
typically follow an unsuccessful screening attempt (i.e., where the screening
cannot rule out the presence of a threat item). In these cases, emergency
response teams on-site will take over and the issue is no longer under PACT's
purview.
The impact of the decision not to load a shipment is limited to the flight for
which the shipment is booked and would only apply in circumstances where
the threat of an explosive device cannot be ruled out. Should information
become available that would clear the flag after the flight has departed, the
cargo would be treated as a new shipment in the system and would be re-
integrated into the supply chain to proceed for transport. The decision to
issue a DNL notice has a very low likelihood of occurrence. As such, the
likelihood of DNL-related delays is also limited. Air carriers’ ability to provide
the PLACI data to PACT as early as practicable prior to the cargo being loaded
onto the aircraft will also help to mitigate potential delays. In this way, air
carriers would help ensure that they are fulfilling their terms of service for
cargo carriage, and that the impacts from any risk mitigation decisions by
PACT remain brief.
Ultimately, decisions to apply a risk mitigation measure to a shipment are
made by PACT analysts in a small number of cases and can take a matter of
seconds to be cleared/reversed. For example, a shipment may be flagged for
review by the system and then cleared by an analyst within seconds without
the need for additional information. The change in status is communicated to
the air carrier in the system immediately following the decision. In the case
where additional information is requested and received, the decision to
prevent the
shipment from being loaded is reversed as soon as possible.
33. The impacts that the decision will have on the rights or freedoms of individuals will likely be:
Little to no impact [ Points: +1 ]
34. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
Shippers who opt to ship and receive cargo through the air cargo supply chain
(i.e., shipping on passenger or all-cargo flights), have and retain the right to
choose this means from among alternative modes to ship cargo (e.g., by
airmail or the marine or land modes (e.g., rail, road)). When a shipper opts to
ship via air cargo, there are terms and conditions that apply to this means of
shipment, and the contract into which they enter with the air carrier stipulates
(pursuant to various conventions) which information is being collected and
shared and that the shipment will be subject to all applicable laws that govern
the shipment of air cargo. Should a shipment be
denied loading, the shipper can invoke their rights to seek remedies under
their contract of carriage or those available under international treaties that
govern liability regimes for the shipment of goods on board aircraft. In the
case where a PACT decision prevents their cargo from being loaded, they will
also be provided contact information for the Program through which they can
request an explanation.
35. The impacts that the decision will have on the equality, dignity, privacy, and autonomy of
individuals will likely be:
Little to no impact [ Points: +1 ]
36. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
A Privacy Impact Assessment is underway and all safeguards are being put in
place to ensure that any personal information that is collected is stored and
disposed of in keeping with all applicable laws in Canada. As the personal
information of shippers collected by the program is limited to the shipper or
consignor’s name, address, and the commodity description, there are few if
any anticipated effects on the equality or dignity of individuals. Additionally,
the use of advanced analytics as part of the risk assessment will significantly
reduce the potential for unintentional bias that could otherwise impact
individuals’ dignity and equality rights. Finally, the decision that is automated
by the PACT algorithmic system will have little to no impact on individuals’
autonomy because the function of the system is limited to the calculation of
the risk of a shipment, and not to its delay or denial of loading. Any decision to
prevent a shipment from being transported by air that may follow from that
calculation of risk (and that could be construed to limit individuals’ autonomy
in opting to use this mode of shipping), remains under the purview of human
analysts who will take into consideration a multitude of factors each time a
shipment is presented. As the risk factors are fluid (e.g. a trade pattern
fluctuates over time), each decision is unique.
37. The impacts that the decision will have on the health and well-being of individuals will likely
be:
Little to no impact [ Points: +1 ]
38. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
The premise of the PACT Program (and others like it around the world) is to
ensure the continued flow of goods around the globe while adding a layer of
security to protect the supply chain from unlawful acts of interference. Using
data to perform a risk assessment on a shipment sometimes weeks in
advance of loading is widely accepted (see the International Civil Aviation
Organization and World Customs Organization's Joint Guiding Principles on
Pre-Loading Advanced Cargo Information) to be an efficient and effective
means to address potential risk in the least obtrusive means available. In the
case of life-saving medical supplies, the supply chain is carefully planned out
and follows predictable routes involving known players. Such shipments are
also clearly labelled and often have specific handling codes that protect
passage from one entity to the next. This allows for a simplified risk
assessment, lessens the chance of shipments being delayed in any way, and
in fact facilitates the safe movement of life-saving materials. If life-saving
materials were to be prevented from being transported on a specific flight, it
would be as a result of a threat item being identified, which if unmitigated,
would endanger lives.
39. The impacts that the decision will have on the economic interests of individuals will likely
be:
Little to no impact [ Points: +1 ]
40. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
A shipper whose shipment is re-screened or prevented from being transported
on the flight could incur additional costs. When a shipper opts to ship via air
cargo, they agree to terms and conditions that apply to its use, including any
applicable fees.
41. The impacts that the decision will have on the ongoing sustainability of an environmental
ecosystem, will likely be:
Little to no impact [ Points: +1 ]
42. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
The PACT Program, including its system design and related decision-making
processes was assessed in accordance with the Cabinet Directive on the
Environmental Assessment of Policy, Plan and Program Proposals. The
Strategic Environmental Assessment (SEA) process was followed and a
Sustainable Transportation Assessment (STA) was completed by Transport
Canada's Environmental Management Branch. The assessment found that the
PACT Program is not expected to result in important environmental effects,
positive or negative.
About the Data - A. Data Source
43. Will the Automated Decision System use personal information as input data?
Yes [ Points: +4 ]
44. Have you verified that the use of personal information is limited to only what is directly
related to delivering a program or service?
Yes [ Points: +0 ]
45. Is the personal information of individuals being used in a decision-making process that
directly affects those individuals?
Yes [ Points: +2 ]
46. Have you verified if the system is using personal information in a way that is consistent
with: (a) the current Personal Information Banks (PIBs) and Privacy Impact Assessments (PIAs)
of your programs or (b) planned or implemented modifications to the PIBs or PIAs that take new
uses and processes into account?
Yes [ Points: +0 ]
47. What is the highest security classification of the input data used by the system? (Select one)
Protected A [ Points: +1 ]
48. Who controls the data?
Private Sector / NGO [ Points: +3 ]
49. Will the system use data from multiple different sources?
Yes [ Points: +4 ]
50. Will the system require input data from an Internet- or telephony-connected device? (e.g.
Internet of Things, sensor)
No [ Points: +0 ]
51. Will the system interface with other IT systems?
Yes [ Points: +4 ]
52. Who collected the data used for training the system?
Your institution [ Points: +1 ]
53. Who collected the input data used by the system?
A foreign government or non-government third party [ Points: +4 ]
54. Please describe the input data collected and used by the system, its source, and method of
collection.
The input data is made up of 8 data elements found on airwaybills: shipper
and consignee names and addresses, commodity description, weight, piece
count, and airwaybill number. This information is shared with Transport
Canada by air carriers through a web API.
About the Data - B. Type of Data
55. Will the system require the analysis of unstructured data to render a recommendation or a
decision?
Yes [ Points: 0 ]
56. What types of unstructured data? (Check all that apply)
Audio and text files [ Points: +2 ]
Section 3.2: Mitigation Questions and Answers
Consultations
1. Internal Stakeholders (federal institutions, including the federal public service)
Yes [ Points: +1 ]
2. Which Internal Stakeholders have you engaged?
Data Governance
Strategic Policy and Planning

Yes [ Points: +1 ]

De-Risking and Mitigation Measures - Privacy
34. If your system uses or creates personal information, have you undertaken a Privacy Impact
Assessment, or updated an existing one?
Yes [ Points: +1 ]
35. Please indicate the following in your answer: Title and scope of the Privacy Impact
Assessment; How the automation project fits into the program; and Date of Privacy Impact
Assessment completion or modification.
The PACT Program’s Privacy Impact Assessment will review the collection,
use, disclosure, and retention of personal information collected as PLACI
under the Canadian Aviation Security Regulations, 2012 (CASR 2012).
Assessments of the privacy and security safeguards applied to the data
repositories that contain the collected PLACI, and of the role and access
controls of TC staff executing the PACT Program are also within the scope of
the PIA.
Through amendments to the CASR 2012, air carriers are required to submit a
specific dataset for all inbound air cargo in support of air cargo security.
Automating part of PACT’s processes allows for the collection, sorting and
risk assessment of 100% of the submitted data. This rate of risk assessment
was not deemed feasible without automation.
The draft PIA is being reviewed by Transport Canada's ATIP division, and the
timeline for its completion aligns with the coming into force of the regulations
in fall 2024.
